summary: Gruber examines a Brand Keys customer loyalty survey showing Apple leading in smartphones, tablets, laptops, online music, and headphones. While noting the survey firm's questionable credibility, he argues Apple's category leadership is largely inarguable regardless. He contends that the real concern isn't whether Apple leads, but whether that dominance breeds complacency. He revisits his nine-year-old argument that Apple benefits from having strong, design-focused competitors, citing Tesla as one of the few worthy rivals—not through direct product competition, but through the battle for top engineering talent.
key_points: Brand Keys survey shows Apple leading customer loyalty in every major consumer electronics category, though the firm's own credibility is questionable
Apple's dominance in tablets and laptops is essentially unchallenged, while Samsung remains a distant second in phones, weakened further by the Note 7 disaster
The real source of anxiety about Apple isn't declining quality but fear that unchallenged leadership leads to complacency
Tesla qualifies as a serious Apple rival not through product overlap but through competing for design and engineering talent
Gruber's 2007 argument that Apple needs more design-focused competition remains relevant a decade later
money_quotes: Who else even could be named the customer loyalty or brand leader in tablets or laptops? No one.

You can make the case for Samsung in phones, but I think most observers would agree that they've always been Pepsi to the iPhone's Coke, and their position is shakier than ever in the wake of the Note 7 fiasco.
I think one reason there's so much consternation today about the state of Apple is simply the fear that their clear leadership in these categories could or already has led to complacency.
I wrote nine years ago that it would be better for Apple if they had more competition from design- and innovation-focused competitors, and I think that remains true today.
That's why I consider Tesla one of Apple's handful of serious rivals, even though they don't yet (and perhaps never will) compete with Apple directly, other than through the recruiting of talent.
key_insight: Apple's greatest risk isn't losing its category leadership but having no worthy competitor to prevent the complacency that unchallenged dominance breeds.
